data.title: Howmet outlines $9.1B revenue target for 2026 amid strong growth in aerospace and gas turbines
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Howmet Aerospace Inc. (HWM) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Plant, Executive Chairman & CEO, highlighted that "Q4 was an extremely solid quarter. Revenue of $2.17 billion was up 15%. Full year revenue was up 11%, and hence, the final quarter saw an acceleration of growth." He further reported, "EBITDA was $653 million, up 29%. Operating income was $580 million, an increase of 34%. Free cash flow after record capital spend of $453 million was $1.43 billion, which is more than $100 million above the guide point and a 93% conversion of net income." Plant also noted $200 million in share buybacks, $50 million in dividends, $55 million for preferred share redemption, and $125 million for debt reduction in the quarter, with a closing cash balance of $743 million.

  • Patrick Winterlich, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "Commercial aerospace growth remained strong throughout 2025, with revenue up 13% in the fourth quarter and up 12% for the full year." He added, "Defense Aerospace growth continued to be robust at 20% in the fourth quarter. Gas turbine growth has been very strong with revenue up 32% in the fourth quarter and up 25% for the full year." Winterlich also commented, "Full year 2025 revenue, EBITDA, EBITDA margin and earnings per share were all records. On a year-over-year basis, revenue was up 11% and EBITDA outpaced revenue growth being up 26%, while absorbing approximately 1,500 net new employees predominantly in the Engine segment."

Outlook

  • John Plant guided for Q1 2026 revenue of $2.235 billion, plus or minus $10 million; EBITDA of $685 million, plus or minus $5 million; and EPS of $1.10, plus or minus $0.01. For full year 2026, guidance is revenue of $9.1 billion, plus or minus $100 million, EBITDA of $2.76 billion, plus or minus $50 million, earnings per share of $4.45, plus or minus $0.01, and free cash flow of $1.6 billion, plus or minus $50 million. Plant remarked, "We remain positive on the growth for 2026 while noting the dependency on aircraft builds."

  • Plant stated the base business of gas turbines "should double in revenue to $2 billion over the next 3 to 5 years."

  • The company completed the acquisition of Brunner Inc. and agreed to pay $1.8 billion for an aerospace fasteners and fittings business, noting the latter's earnings impact will not be material until deal closure.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenue was $2.17 billion, EBITDA was $653 million, and operating income was $580 million. Full year revenue reached $8.15 billion, EBITDA was $2.42 billion, and free cash flow was $1.43 billion. Capital expenditures for the year were $453 million.

  • Segment results included Engine Products revenue of $1.16 billion in Q4, up 20%, and commercial aerospace segment revenue up 17%. Fastening Systems revenue increased 13% to $454 million in Q4. Engineered Structures revenue rose 4% to $287 million, and Forged Wheels quarterly revenue was up 9%.

  • The company noted a 93% free cash flow conversion of net income and improved net debt to trailing EBITDA, ending the year at a record low of 1x.

Q&A

  • Douglas Harned, Bernstein: Asked about the evolving outlook for engine products and margins. John Plant answered, "I think the constant throughout this starts off with commercial aerospace, where I've been convinced that growth will be robust and continuing." Plant explained that the gas turbine market has shifted from cyclical to more consistent, driven by data center and natural gas demand, and described ongoing capital investments and yield improvement efforts. On margins, Plant stated, "I never willing to consider what margins are for the future because I find it always a very difficult topic to cover."

  • Unknown Analyst (for Seth Seifman, JPMorgan): Inquired about minimal improvement in revenue, EBITDA, and EPS guidance for the rest of 2026 and margin implications. Plant responded, "We do have an extraordinary amount going on... we're deploying capital for new equipment at an extraordinary rate. We're building -- we're extending 5 new manufacturing plants." He emphasized the balancing act of execution across multiple projects.

  • Robert Stallard, Vertical Research: Questioned ROIC for new investments. Plant replied, "There essentially is no difference between the margin that we have on gas turbines and output in our commercial aerospace or defense aerospace."

  • John Godyn, Citi: Asked about capital deployment priorities. Plant responded, "First priority... is always the deployment of capital to enable the growth opportunities that we have... We're investing in the business at record levels... We're deploying share buyback in a significant way."

  • Scott Deuschle, Deutsche Bank: Asked if gas turbine revenue could surpass commercial jet engine revenue. Plant answered, "I don't think so because I think our commercial aerospace and our defense aerospace business is also growing rapidly."

  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Requested more detail on recent acquisitions. Plant described CAM as expanding the fittings and couplings segment and Brunner as enabling entry into longer and wider fastener products.

  • Louis Raffetto, Wolfe Research: Sought color on spares performance. Plant reported, "Our spares business grew over 30%, probably getting close to 33% for the year... We were at 21% for the 2025 year."

  • Peter Arment, Baird: Asked about automation progress. Plant described significant automation investments in recent years but noted engineering capacity is currently focused on customer and market expansions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised questions on growth sustainability, margins, capital allocation, and the pace of investments, reflecting a neutral to slightly positive tone with probing for risks related to execution and capital deployment.

  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing record results and growth investments, but became more measured in Q&A, balancing optimism with caution on margin predictability and execution risks. John Plant frequently acknowledged the complexity and challenge ahead, saying, "it's always struggle to believe you'll be successful on every single one of them."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment remained probing but slightly more focused on execution risk due to the scale of investments and acquisitions, while management continued to express confidence but with increased emphasis on the challenges from capacity expansion and integration.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Current quarter guidance for 2026 revenue is $9.1 billion compared to the previous quarter's $9 billion, reflecting a small upward revision. Q1 2026 revenue guidance is $2.235 billion, up from Q4 2025.

  • Capital deployment escalated, with additional acquisitions and a larger share buyback authorization. Management highlighted more aggressive growth investments, including further plant expansions and new acquisitions.

  • Segment performance remained strong, with Engine Products and Fastening Systems showing continued growth, while commercial transportation volumes stabilized at a low level.

  • Management's tone shifted slightly toward caution on execution risk, relative to the previous quarter's more upbeat outlook.

  • Analyst focus shifted from market opportunity and margin expansion to questions around integration, capital allocation, and the scalability of recent investments.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited execution risk with multiple simultaneous plant expansions, acquisitions, and hiring. Plant noted, "it's always struggle to believe you'll be successful on every single one of them."

  • Spares growth, while robust, is not explicitly forecasted to continue at the current pace.

  • Exposure to aircraft build rates and regulatory timelines, especially in commercial truck wheels, was noted.

  • Analysts voiced concerns about capital allocation discipline, returns on recent acquisitions, and margin sustainability as capacity comes online.

Final Takeaway

Howmet Aerospace enters 2026 with record financial performance, ambitious growth targets, and a slate of new investments, including plant expansions and acquisitions. Management guided for $9.1 billion in revenue for 2026, supported by robust demand in commercial aerospace, defense, and gas turbines, with gas turbine revenue targeted to double over the next three to five years. While the company is executing on multiple fronts—organic growth, M&A, and capital returns—leadership emphasized both the opportunities and the operational complexity ahead, with a continued commitment to balancing margin, growth, and free cash flow.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Bob's Discount Furniture moves to a post-IPO high as investors see upside
data.description:
Bob's Discount Furniture (BOBS) rose to a new post-IPO high of $20.30 on Thursday before falling back. The company priced its IPO last week at $17 per share, which was at the bottom end of the expected range of $17 to $19.

Bob’s generated net income of $80.7M for the nine-month period that ended on September 28, off revenue of $1.72B. Notably, comparable sales growth for the period was +9%.

Looking ahead, Bob’s Discount Furniture (BOBS) is pursuing an aggressive physical-store growth strategy while also positioning itself for omnichannel competition. Industry insiders said the IPO filing signals an intent to fund further growth plans.

data.title: Financial stress accelerates to record highs, says the National Foundation for Credit Counseling
data.description:
Financial stress is accelerating beyond previous records, according to the National Foundation for Credit Counseling (or NFCC), which released its Q1 2026 Financial Stress forecast.

After returning to a peak of 6.5 in Q4 2025, the NFCC forecasts stress will climb to a new historic high of 6.8 in the first quarter of 2026.

The Federal Reserve’s report confirmed that total household debt has swelled to $18.8T, but the NFCC’s data suggests the situation is even more dire.

According to the NFCC, an increasing number of counseled consumers are “technically insolvent”—they have income, but “no remaining disposable cash flow to fund a more affordably structured repayment plan after covering basic necessities.”

“The Fed’s data tells us who missed a payment yesterday; our forecast of 6.8 tells us who is going to hit the wall tomorrow,” said Mike Croxson, CEO of the NFCC. “We are seeing a disturbing shift from discretionary debt to survival debt. When the financial buffer runs out, the climb in stress isn’t gradual. It’s vertical.”

Key insights from the NFCC forecast reveal troubling patterns in household finances. The Financial Stress Forecast held at 6.6 for three consecutive quarters—a sign, the NFCC notes, that “high financial stress has become the new normal for middle-income households.”

The organization also identified a “functional tightening” of credit, explaining that high interest rates and inflation “have raised the barrier to entry so high that distressed borrowers are being priced out of the lifelines they need most.”

The NFCC’s report also highlights what it calls “invisible distress” that traditional credit reports miss entirely.

According to the forecast, traditional credit reporting models fail to capture the full picture because consumers are “prioritizing credit card payments over other obligations to maintain liquidity, masking their true financial fragility until the moment of default.”

The NFCC Financial Stress Forecast is designed specifically to identify stress before it results in charge-offs, providing an early warning system for household financial instability.

More on the U.S. economy

  • The markets will see 4 rate cuts this year – Goldman Sachs’ Jonny Fine
  • Economic data does not support aggressive rate cuts – Former Fed Vice Chair Roger Ferguson
  • Nonfarm payrolls growth shocks to the upside in January; unemployment slips to 4.3%
  • The economy is not ‘crying out for interest rate cuts’ – Goldman Sachs’s Jan Hatzius
  • The Fed will cut interest rates ‘a whole bunch of times…substantially more than two’ – Greenlight Capital’s David Einhorn

data.title: Anthropic raises $30B at $380B valuation, includes money from Microsoft, Nvidia, others
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) confirmed on Thursday that it has closed its oft-speculated latest funding round, raising more money than expected.

The maker of the Claude family of artificial intelligence models raised $30B in a Series G funding round at a $380B valuation.

The round was co-led by D. E. Shaw Ventures, Dragoneer, Founders Fund, ICONIQ, and MGX, Anthropic said in a statement.  It also includes several asset managers, hedge funds, and venture capital firms, as well as some of the previous money that came from Microsoft (MSFT) and Nvidia (NVDA).

Anthropic said the money will be used for "the frontier research, product development, and infrastructure expansions that have made Anthropic the market leader in enterprise AI and coding."

“Whether it is entrepreneurs, startups, or the world’s largest enterprises, the message from our customers is the same: Claude is increasingly becoming more critical to how businesses work,” said Krishna Rao, Anthropic’s Chief Financial Officer. “This fundraising reflects the incredible demand we are seeing from these customers, and we will use this investment to continue building the enterprise-grade products and models they have come to depend on.”

In addition, Anthropic said that the company's revenue run rate is now $14B, and this figure has grown more than 10 times annually over the past three years. Anthropic's revenue run rate surpassed $9B at the end of 2025, according to previous reporting.

Included in that is a surge in Claude Code, the company's agentic AI coding tool. Claude Code's revenue run rate is now more than $2.5B and has more than doubled since the start of the year, Anthropic added. Weekly active users for Claude Code have also doubled since Jan. 1, Anthropic explained.

"Business subscriptions to Claude Code have quadrupled since the start of 2026, and enterprise use has grown to represent over half of all Claude Code revenue," Anthropic said.

Anthropic, which competes with OpenAI (OPENAI) and xAI (X.AI), has also reportedly been working with advisors in advance of a potential initial public offering later this year.

data.title: AMG targets 30% growth in fee-related earnings for Q1 2026 while advancing alternative AUM
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Affiliated Managers Group, Inc. (AMG) (AMG) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jay Horgen, CEO & Director, reported "outstanding results in 2025, one of the strongest years in our company's history, with record annual economic earnings per share and substantial organic growth, including record net inflows and alternative strategies." He highlighted $26.05 in full year economic earnings per share, a 22% increase year-over-year, and approximately $29 billion in annual net client cash flows, with an organic growth rate of 4%.
  • Horgen announced AMG added approximately $97 billion in alternative assets under management in 2025, a 35% increase, alongside $700 million in share repurchases, representing 11% of shares outstanding.
  • He detailed more than $1 billion allocated in capital across five new investments and highlighted a new partnership with HighBrook, as well as an incremental minority investment in Garda. Horgen stated, "Both investments are consistent with our strategy and are expected to be accretive to our earnings in 2026."
  • A management transition was disclosed, with Tom Wojcik, President & COO, announcing his departure. Horgen acknowledged Wojcik's contributions, stating, "Tom has informed us that he is ready to take a next step in his career and will leave AMG to pursue other leadership opportunities."
  • Dava Ritchea, CFO, stated, "In the fourth quarter, we reported adjusted EBITDA of $378 million, which grew 34% year-over-year and included $125 million in net performance fee earnings." She emphasized strong share repurchases, capital deployment, and a simplified balance sheet following refinancing activities.

Outlook

  • Ritchea shared first quarter 2026 guidance: "We expect adjusted EBITDA to be in the range of $310 million to $330 million based on current AUM levels, reflecting our market blend, which was up 3% quarter-to-date as of February 11 and including net performance fees of $40 million to $60 million."
  • Guidance for first quarter economic earnings per share is set between $7.98 and $8.52, with a share count of 27.4 million. Ritchea added, "Q1 fee-related earnings guidance of $270 million... represents 30% expected growth in quarterly fee-related earnings versus Q1 2025."
  • Ritchea stated, "We expect net performance fee earnings of approximately $170 million for 2026 which is consistent with our 5-year average from 2021 to 2025."

Financial Results

  • AMG reported adjusted EBITDA of $378 million for Q4 2025 and $1.1 billion for the full year, up 11% versus 2024. Fee-related earnings grew 20% year-over-year for the quarter and 8% for the full year.
  • Economic earnings per share were $9.48 for Q4 and $26.05 for the full year 2025, with a 45% year-over-year growth in Q4 and 22% for the year.
  • Share repurchases totaled $700 million for 2025, with $350 million repurchased in Q4—the largest quarterly amount in firm history.
  • Significant transactions included receiving more than $730 million in pretax distributions and sale proceeds from liquidity events across three affiliates, achieving more than 2.5x invested capital and an average IRR of over 35%.

Q&A

  • Daniel Fannon, Jefferies: Asked about AQR's outlook and diversity of flows. Horgen responded, "There are 2 standouts... the average fee rates that they're coming in from the private markets and from the liquid alternatives is higher than our average fee rates." Ritchea added, "AQR really has a decades-long track record of true innovation, product differentiation, portfolio diversification and strong performance... as they continue to innovate, they've expanded their reach with both institutional investors and the largest gatekeepers of U.S. wealth assets."
  • Alexander Blostein, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about the private side pipeline and organic growth. Horgen discussed Pantheon's specialization and product structures for U.S. and non-U.S. investors. Wojcik described a new partnership with Brown Brothers for structured credit and AMG's strategy to seed and scale new products.
  • William Katz, TD Cowen: Sought clarity on global wealth management growth. Horgen explained, "We are seeing significant growth in wealth... it's primarily alternatives." Ritchea noted, "When we think about accessing that wealth channel, both within AMG, but also at our affiliates, it's important to think about those 2 pieces together."
  • Brian Bedell, Deutsche Bank: Asked about AQR's EBITDA contribution and performance fees trajectory. Ritchea stated, "AQR was a double-digit contributor to EBITDA in 2025. And we do expect that to grow into 2026 and expect them to be north of 20% this year." She outlined, "We expect net performance fee earnings of approximately $170 million for 2026."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about AMG's momentum, growth in alternatives, and the outlook for AQR and Pantheon, with a focus on clarity for future organic growth and product innovation.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing strong business fundamentals and growth prospects. Phrases like "we are confident that the best is yet to come" and "we are very excited about what we can accomplish over the next 5 years" signaled high confidence.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both management and analysts exhibited increased optimism, with management reinforcing discipline and commitment to long-term value creation.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 adjusted EBITDA increased compared to prior quarters, with 30% expected growth in fee-related earnings versus Q1 2025.
  • Strategic focus has shifted further toward alternatives, highlighted by increases in alternative AUM and new partnerships.
  • Analysts continued to focus on organic growth drivers, particularly in alternatives, and sought updates on AQR and Pantheon's contributions.
  • Management's tone remained confident, with greater emphasis on innovation, product development, and distribution expansion compared to the previous quarter.
  • Key metrics such as adjusted EBITDA, economic earnings per share, and share repurchases all showed year-over-year growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing outflows in active equities, with net outflows of approximately $12 billion in Q4 and $45 billion for the year, reflecting industry headwinds.
  • Ritchea mentioned, "Multi-asset and fixed income was flat for both the quarter and the year."
  • Analysts probed on competitive pressures in distribution and the sustainability of performance fees, with management citing diversification and product innovation as mitigation.
  • The transition of President & COO Tom Wojcik was noted, but management expressed confidence in the depth of the leadership team.

Final Takeaway

AMG management highlighted a record year for growth in alternatives, significant organic net inflows, and robust capital allocation through investments and share repurchases. Guidance points to strong momentum entering 2026, with expectations for continued expansion in alternatives, product innovation, and earnings growth. The company sees itself well positioned for further value creation, leveraging its differentiated partnership model and disciplined strategy, with management expressing high confidence in delivering on long-term objectives.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Exelon outlines $41.3B capital plan and projects 8% rate base growth through 2029 while targeting $2.81–$2.91 EPS for 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Exelon Corporation (EXC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Calvin Butler, CEO, President & Director, highlighted Exelon's strong operational and financial performance, stating, "For 2025, we reported adjusted operating earnings per share of $2.77, delivering above expectations. This continues our track record of exceeding the midpoint of guidance in each year as a stand-alone utility. And since 2021, we've achieved a 7.4% annual earnings growth rate and 8% rate base growth through 2025, highlighting our ability to navigate changes and consistently execute." Butler underscored Exelon's industry-leading reliability and noted that the utilities maintained "top-quartile reliability metrics once again, and we're ranked 1, 2, 4 and 7 amongst our peers based on 2024 benchmarking data." He emphasized the company's investments in grid resilience, referencing the response to Winter Storm Fern where "fewer than 1% of our customers experienced outages even as an extreme weather impacted our regions." Butler also addressed regulatory progress, mentioning "final settlements for the Atlantic City Electric and Delmarva gas rate cases, reconciliation orders at ComEd and BGE, and the filing of ComEd's second multiyear grid plan."
  • Butler announced an updated capital plan: "Looking ahead, we now expect to invest $41.3 billion of capital to support our customers, with more than 70% of the plan-over-plan increase driven by transmission." He further stated, "With continued returns on equity in the 9% to 10% range, we expect rate base growth of approximately 8% and annualized earnings growth of 5% to 7% through 2029, with the expectation of being near the top end of that range."
  • Jeanne Jones, Executive VP of Audit & Risk and CFO, reported, "In 2025, we delivered $2.73 per share on a GAAP basis and $2.77 per share on a non-GAAP basis for the full year, reflecting strong year-over-year growth. For the quarter, Exelon earned $0.58 on a GAAP basis and $0.59 on a non-GAAP basis. Full year earnings above our guidance range primarily benefited from favorable weather and storm conditions and the resolution of certain regulatory proceedings."

Outlook

  • Butler announced, "For 2026, we are initiating operating earnings guidance of $2.81 to $2.91 per share." Jones added, "With much of our growth aligned with completed rate cases and continued strong cost management, the 2026 implied midpoint relative to the midpoint of our 2025 estimated guidance range is ahead of previous disclosures, reflecting midpoint-to-midpoint growth above 6%." Management projects "annualized earnings growth of 5% to 7% through 2029, with the expectation of being near the top end of that range."

Financial Results

  • Exelon delivered $2.73 per share on a GAAP basis and $2.77 per share on a non-GAAP basis for 2025, with Q4 earnings of $0.58 GAAP and $0.59 non-GAAP per share. Jones noted, "Full year earnings above our guidance range primarily benefited from favorable weather and storm conditions and the resolution of certain regulatory proceedings."
  • The company is planning to invest almost $10 billion in 2026 and $41.3 billion over the next four years, representing a $3.3 billion or 9% increase from the prior four-year planning period. About 70% of the additional capex is attributable to incremental transmission investments, which management links to increased demand for high-voltage investments and capacity expansion.
  • Exelon issued $1 billion in convertible debt in December and has already secured 20% of its planned equity needs for the next four years.

Q&A

  • Nicholas Campanella, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: Questioned how Exelon expects to remain at the high end of its 5% to 7% earnings outlook given financing lag. Jeanne Jones responded that "if you look at kind of the equity needs, that sort of assume an average debt cost, but then I think what you might be missing is the AFUDC associated with transmission capital."
  • Campanella asked about regulatory strategy in Pennsylvania and Maryland. Butler said, "with affordability at front and center in all of our jurisdictions, we lean into that first. But we also recognize that we have to maintain a reliable and resilient grid."
  • Campanella inquired about supply challenges and the PJM RBA process. Colette Honorable, Executive VP of Compliance, Chief Legal Officer & Corporate Secretary, explained, "We need to focus on supply because we know it will lower customer electric costs. We know that we will also see improved reliability."
  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, Research Division: Asked about legislative collaboration in Pennsylvania and other jurisdictions. Butler replied, "we understand where Governor Shapiro was coming from because we're all frustrated with the affordability... So to your direct question, is there an opportunity to have conversations and engage with? Absolutely."
  • Pourreza sought clarity on the step-function change in growth trajectory. Jones stated, "last rate base CAGR was 7.4%, we're sitting at 7.9% now... you should feel confident in that growth, but it is continuing to progress."
  • Paul Zimbardo, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Questioned conservatism in growth guidance. Jones responded, "financing costs are increasing, right? So you've got to account for that. But we are investing more in transmission."
  • Steven Fleishman, Wolfe Research, LLC: Asked about transmission-driven ROE movement. Jones said, "To your point, as we have been turning the ship towards transmission, I think you can expect that, if not slightly better."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a slightly positive tone, pressing for details on growth drivers, regulatory plans, and financing strategy while expressing appreciation for consistent execution.
  • Management remained confident and disciplined in their responses, emphasizing "we are confident we will execute this next stage of growth" and repeatedly affirming a commitment to exceeding expectations.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone remained confident and forward-looking, with analysts maintaining a constructive but probing approach.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Operating earnings exceeded the previous guidance range for 2025, compared to reaffirmed guidance in Q3.
  • The capital plan increased by $3.3 billion, with a sharper focus on transmission investments than previously outlined.
  • Management now expects rate base growth of 8% through 2029, versus 7.4% through 2028 stated last quarter.
  • Guidance language shifted to emphasize mid-to-high end growth targets and a more actionable, diversified capital plan.
  • Analysts' focus remained on growth sustainability, regulatory outcomes, and incremental upside from transmission and large load projects.
  • Management’s confidence in execution and delivering at or above guidance midpoint persisted from last quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted ongoing supply-side challenges and high customer supply costs.
  • Regulatory timing and outcomes, particularly in Maryland and Pennsylvania, were noted as areas of uncertainty.
  • Financing costs and execution of large-scale capital investments remain under watch, with Jones stating, "we want to maintain and keep that cushion we've worked so hard to get on the balance sheet."
  • Analysts raised concerns about the potential for legislative delays and the ability to coordinate among diverse stakeholders.

Final Takeaway

Exelon’s management reinforced a disciplined execution strategy, spotlighting above-guidance earnings for 2025, an expanded $41.3 billion capital plan focusing on transmission, and an 8% rate base growth target through 2029. With a 2026 operating EPS guidance of $2.81 to $2.91, the company maintains its commitment to balanced funding, strong reliability, and proactive stakeholder engagement, positioning itself for continued growth and value delivery over the coming years.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: West Pharmaceutical targets 5%–7% organic revenue growth for 2026 as HVP demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Eric Green reported "another solid quarter with fourth quarter revenues, adjusted EPS and cash flow coming in above our expectations," highlighting that the company "surpassed the $3 billion mark in net sales, achieving year-over-year organic growth of over 4%." He outlined the achievement of 8% adjusted earnings per share growth and a 70% increase in free cash flow for the year. Green emphasized that "growth was fueled by increasing demand for high-value product components" and cited key drivers: the rise of biologics and biosimilars, increased global regulatory requirements such as Annex 1, and the expanding GLP-1 market.

  • Green stated the launch of the West Synchrony prefillable syringe system "marks a significant shift in drug delivery solutions" and noted the sale of the SmartDose 3.5mL business aligns with their focus on "patient-centric approach for large on-body delivery devices."

  • The CEO announced that the Contract Manufacturing segment commenced commercial production in Dublin and highlighted that "5 out of the 10 members" of the executive leadership team joined in the last 12 months.

  • CFO Robert McMahon reported, "Q4 revenue increased 7.5% on a reported basis and grew 3.3% organically," with HVP components delivering $390 million in revenue and growing 15.1% organically. He also stated, "Gross margin was 37.8% in the quarter, up 130 basis points year-over-year," and "free cash flow to $469 million for the year, up 70% year-on-year."

Outlook

  • Management guided for 2026 revenue in the range of $3.215 billion to $3.275 billion, reflecting reported growth of 4.6% to 6.5% and organic growth of 5% to 7%. HVP components are expected to "grow high single digit to low double digits organically for the year."

  • McMahon explained, "We expect the non-GLP-1 HVP components to drive the majority of growth, accounting for 4 of the 5 points of growth." He added, "GLP-1s to grow in 2026, albeit at a slower pace than in 2025. To get to our midpoint, we'd expect GLP-1s to grow roughly 10% year-on-year."

  • Adjusted earnings per share for 2026 is forecast to be between $7.85 to $8.20, with first quarter 2026 revenue expected to be between $770 million and $790 million and adjusted diluted earnings per share between $1.65 and $1.70.

Financial Results

  • Q4 revenues were $805 million, up 7.5% reported and 3.3% organic. Adjusted operating margins reached 21.4%. Adjusted EPS for Q4 was $2.04, up 12% over the prior year. Free cash flow in Q4 was $175 million.

  • HVP components, representing 48% of total net sales, grew over 15% in Q4. HVP delivery devices posted $110 million in revenue; standard products declined 1.7% organically; contract manufacturing revenues increased 1.9% organically.

  • Operating cash flow for the year was $755 million, and capital expenditures totaled $286 million, with a planned step down to $250–$275 million in 2026.

Q&A

  • Michael Ryskin, BofA Securities: Asked if the 10% GLP-1 growth guidance is conservative. McMahon responded, "We have not seen any changes in our customer behavior... What I would characterize that is as a very conservative start to our initial guidance."

  • Ryskin follow-up on capital deployment. Green stated, "Our first priority... does not take our eye off the organic growth," but noted interest in accretive technologies. McMahon added, "Just on the last piece regarding returning cash to shareholders, that is something that we're actively discussing."

  • Daniel Markowitz, Evercore ISI: Queried about demand outstripping supply for HVP components. Green explained, "The demand continues to outpace our supply," and McMahon noted, "We're continuing to add capacity beyond what our expectations were... that's a good sign."

  • Justin Bowers, Deutsche Bank: Asked about new GLP-1 molecules and HVP component configurations. Green responded, "There are consistency of new molecules being developed that would use similar elastomeric HVP components that we currently supply for the GLP-1 space."

  • Paul Knight, KeyBanc: Inquired about ramp-up stages at Grand Rapids and Dublin. Green described Grand Rapids as "closer to... peak volumes in the first part of 2026," while Dublin is "still in the ramp-up phase" with commercial drug handling operations commencing.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' tone was neutral to slightly positive, with focus on growth guidance conservatism, supply-demand dynamics, and capital deployment. No overt skepticism was noted.

  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks conveyed confidence and momentum, while Q&A responses were measured and emphasized prudence, especially around GLP-1 growth guidance—"What I would characterize that is as a very conservative start to our initial guidance."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, confidence in growth drivers was more pronounced, while analysts remained focused on sustainability of growth and operational execution.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 increased in specificity, with explicit 5%–7% organic growth target vs. prior quarter's general positive outlook.

  • Strategic clarity improved with the sale of SmartDose and launch of Synchrony.

  • Analysts continued to probe sustainability of HVP growth and supply constraints, but management reiterated strong demand and capacity expansion efforts.

  • Margin expansion guidance was strengthened, with a target of over 100 basis points in 2026, compared to previous commentary on returning to margin growth.

  • Management’s confidence on biologics, GLP-1, and Annex 1 as drivers remained intact, but with more detailed breakdowns.

Risks and Concerns

  • Temporary production disruption at the Arizona facility negatively impacted contract manufacturing segment; facility is now operational.

  • Demand for HVP components outstripping supply, but management is investing in capacity and tech transfers to meet demand.

  • Tariff landscape assumed stable; management stated they have "effectively covered that impact" for now.

  • Ongoing need to ramp capacity in Europe and U.S. to avoid future supply-demand imbalances.

Final Takeaway

Management emphasized that West Pharmaceutical Services enters 2026 with strong momentum, underpinned by secular growth drivers in biologics, Annex 1 regulatory upgrades, and GLP-1 demand. The company expects 5%–7% organic revenue growth and double-digit EPS expansion, with HVP components as the primary engine. Investments in capacity, the launch of Synchrony, and portfolio optimization efforts position the company for continued margin expansion and robust cash generation in the coming year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: L'Oréal Q4 sales, operating profits miss expectations
data.description:
• Shares of L'Oréal (LRLCY) (LRLCF) are underwater on Thursday after reporting disappointing fourth-quarter operating profit, sales, and like-for-like ((LFL)) sales growth, as well as total sales for FY25.
  • Investors shrugged off double-digit growth in e-commerce and another year of record gross and operating margins.
  • Of note was the second-half recovery in its two largest markets, the U.S. and China, while continuing its “emerging conquest.”
  • However, a softer-than-expected operating profit of €8.89B missed €8.9B estimates, and LFL sales growth of 6% was slower than 6.46% estimates.
  • Additionally, the company’s Q4 and FY25 sales of €11.25B and €44.05B, respectively, were both below expectations.
  • Shares are down more than 7%, breaching support at the 100- and 200-day moving averages.

data.title: Genfit adds $20M as Ipsen records strong sales for liver drug
data.description:
Genfit (GNFTY) (GNFTF) closed higher in Europe on Thursday after the French biotech said that it is entitled to a $20M payment from partner Ipsen (IPSEY) due to the stronger-than-expected commercial performance of Iqirvo, their jointly developed liver drug.

In 2024, under the FDA’s accelerated pathway for approvals. Ipsen (IPSEF) received the U.S. nod for the oral, once-daily therapy as part of a combination regimen for primary biliary cholangitis ((PBC)), an autoimmune disorder affecting bile ducts.

Ipsen (IPSEY), which reported its Q4 results on Thursday, posted $88M and $208M in net sales for Iqirvo in PBC for the quarter and full year, respectively.

The better-than-expected sales performance has led to the first commercial milestone payment under a licensing deal signed by the companies in 2021, Genfit (GNFT) said, adding that the payment occurred ahead of its initial plans.

Additionally, the company said that Ipsen (IPSEY) confirmed the initiation of a Phase 3 trial for the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor ((PPAR)) agonist against primary sclerosing cholangitis, a rare liver disease with no approved therapies.

“We are pleased with Ipsen’s continued success with Iqirvo,” Genfit (GNFT) CEO Pascal Prigent remarked, adding, “We believe that this commercial trajectory has the potential to be transformative for our business model.”

data.title: Granite Construction targets 6%–8% organic growth in 2026 as acquisitions and margin expansion drive outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Granite Construction Incorporated (GVA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Kyle Larkin stated that "In 2025, we continue to focus on bidding and building the right projects, investing in our materials business and expanding our geographic footprint through targeted M&A." Larkin highlighted a disciplined approach to project selection, emphasizing high-quality bid build opportunities in home markets. The company grew its committed and awarded projects (CAP) to a record $7 billion at year-end 2025, the highest in its history. He pointed to a strategy shift since 2020, which has increased Construction segment gross profit margin from 8.8% in 2020 to 15.7% in 2025, with organic top line growth. Larkin also noted ongoing investments in the Materials segment, citing a jump in cash gross profit from 19% in 2023 to 26% in 2025, and confirmed plans to spend another $50 million in strategic CapEx in the materials business in 2026. Three 2025 acquisitions—Warren Paving, Papich Construction, and Cinderlite—expanded the company’s Southeast platform and home markets in California and Nevada. Larkin described these as "margin accretive acquisitions and strong growing markets" and indicated acquisition activity would remain a major growth component in 2026.
  • Larkin said, "We believe we are on track to achieve our 2027 financial targets supported by favorable market conditions, robust infrastructure funding and consistent execution across the business."
  • CFO Staci Woolsey reported, "2025 was a tremendous year of growth with year-over-year increases in a number of areas. Revenue increased 10% to $4.4 billion. Gross profit increased 24% to $711 million, adjusted net income increased 29% to $276 million. Adjusted EBITDA increased 31% to $527 million and operating cash flow increased 3% to $469 million."

Outlook

  • The company expects 2026 revenue in the range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion, which includes a full year of 2025 acquisitions. Woolsey stated, "We expect revenue to grow to a range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion. This reflects our record CAP balance and the strong macro environment and places organic growth at the high end of our 2027 target CAGR of 6% to 8%."
  • Adjusted EBITDA margin for 2026 is projected in the range of 12% to 13% of revenue. Woolsey explained, "With our high-quality CAP portfolio, strong market and high-performing materials business, we expect continued adjusted EBITDA margin expansion, in line with our 2027 financial target of 12.5% to 14.5% of revenue."
  • SG&A is targeted at 8.5% to 9% of revenue, including $48 million in stock-based compensation expense. The company plans CapEx of $140 million to $160 million, with about $50 million allocated to the materials segment.

Financial Results

  • Revenue for 2025 reached $4.4 billion, up 10% year-over-year. Gross profit rose 24% to $711 million. Adjusted net income was $276 million and adjusted EBITDA $527 million, with operating cash flow of $469 million.
  • Construction segment revenue in Q4 increased $119 million or 14% year-over-year to $940 million. Newly acquired companies contributed $59 million in Construction segment revenue. Construction segment gross profit reached $143 million, with a segment gross profit margin of 15%.
  • Materials segment revenue increased $69 million year-over-year to $225 million; gross profit for the segment rose by $2 million to $25 million. Materials cash gross profit for the quarter climbed $10 million year-over-year to $47 million or 21% of revenue. Full-year cash gross profit margin improved 490 basis points year-over-year to 26%.
  • The company ended 2025 with $650 million in cash and marketable securities and $1.3 billion in debt.

Q&A

  • Brent Thielman, D.A. Davidson: Asked about federal infrastructure funding and the outlook post-IIJA expiration. Larkin responded, "There's still a really nice runway of spending to go. So that will last likely for a few more years. I think what we hear really from industry today is that there's still bipartisan support... the investment amount is significantly higher... than what is in the IIJA."
  • Thielman followed up on direct federal opportunities and border infrastructure. Larkin explained, "We actually have one contract today in Southeastern Texas... just under $200 million. And we started that work last November. So there is a huge program and opportunity in front of us." He clarified, "We don't have any additional border infrastructure work in the guidance that we provided you today."
  • Steven Ramsey, Thompson Research Group: Inquired about CAP targets and dependency on current levels. Larkin said, "The $7 billion is a really high-quality CAP. The margin profile within our CAP continues to improve, and that's going to also get us to those 2027 targets."
  • Ramsey asked about strategic CapEx allocation and Warren integration. Woolsey said, "That's more heavily weighted towards the legacy business and expanding reserves and doing automation projects there... Warren Paving is off to a strong start... all 3 of our acquisitions last year are performing very well."
  • Kevin Gainey, Thompson, Davis: Asked about project bidding opportunities by vertical. Larkin responded, "The market is strong. It's been strong. It remains strong... The public market with the IIJA is still a big part of our business, around 85% or more today."
  • Gainey followed up on operational concerns with high CAP. Larkin said, "We don't have any execution concerns at all in that regard."
  • Gainey asked about EBITDA guidance range. Larkin cited weather, execution, and new work wins as factors, stating, "We tend to outperform our projects more than we underperform today."
  • Michael Dudas, Vertical Research Partners: Asked about best value contract backlog and future visibility. Larkin said, "As more states pass legislation to allow CMGC or CMAR or progressive design build, we could see that increase. And I think that's okay. It allows us to do some more complex larger contracts in a derisked manner."
  • Dudas asked about materials pricing. Larkin projected "mid-single-digit price improvements on the ag side and low single digit on the asphalt" for 2026. On cost control, he added, "Costs year-over-year actually been flat... automation efforts we put in place, the standardization of our materials playbook."
  • Adam Bubes, Goldman Sachs: Probed margin expansion drivers and M&A pipeline. Larkin broke down margin expansion between construction and materials, noting, "It's about a 70 basis point improvement between Construction and Materials... about a 50 basis point improvement on SG&A... nets out to about a 50 basis point improvement." On M&A, he noted, "I do expect and we expect to get several things done this year."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on the sustainability of growth, CAP quality, federal infrastructure funding, and integration/execution risk; the tone was neutral to slightly positive, with multiple questions on guidance clarity and margin drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone in prepared remarks and Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing operational discipline, positive acquisition integration, and strong market conditions. Larkin stated, "I have strong confidence in the future of Granite."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, confidence remained high but Q4 featured more detailed discussion of execution risks and margin levers, with management addressing analyst questions directly and providing specific forward-looking commentary.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was updated to a revenue range of $4.9 billion to $5.1 billion for 2026, compared to the prior quarter’s annual target of $4.35 billion to $4.45 billion for 2025. Adjusted EBITDA margin guidance for 2026 was set at 12% to 13%, up from 11.5% to 12.5% for 2025.
  • Strategic focus shifted further to M&A-driven growth, with the Warren Paving, Papich Construction, and Cinderlite acquisitions emphasized as outperforming expectations. The CAP reached a record $7 billion, up from $6.3 billion at Q3’s end.
  • Analysts’ questions advanced from acquisition execution to in-depth margin drivers, federal project pipeline, and CAP conversion. Management continued to express confidence, with slightly more discussion of execution and risk management in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged weather as a potential risk to execution and margin realization, especially in Q1 and Q4. Larkin noted, "weather can always be an opportunity or it can be a hindrance for us."
  • The risk profile for federal border infrastructure contracts was noted as changing, prompting a more disciplined approach to project pursuit.
  • No operational concerns were expressed regarding executing on the $7 billion CAP, and management described cost control measures and integration efforts as robust.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored that Granite Construction is executing on its strategy of focused project selection, disciplined M&A, and investment in high-margin materials businesses. With a record CAP, strong cash generation, and a proven track record of integrating acquisitions, the company expects to achieve organic revenue growth at the upper end of its 6%–8% target in 2026, expand margins, and deliver on its 2027 financial goals, all while maintaining a confident outlook on both public and private market opportunities.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
